Author: Owen Zanzal
Title: The Great Unlock: The Rise of Personal Software
Text:
The Great Unlock: The Rise of Personal Software
The Conversation We Are Having vs The Shift That Is Happening
Scroll through any tech feed right now and you’ll find the same anxious chorus: productivity is collapsing, AI is generating slop code, engineers are burning out, SaaS spend is spiraling. The metrics obsession runs deep — ticket velocity, cost per engineer, lines shipped per sprint. Entire organizations have become optimization machines for team output.
But what if we’re measuring the wrong layer entirely?
Beneath the noise about whether AI makes teams 10% or 40% faster, something more fundamental is shifting. Call it The Great Unlock: individuals are gaining the ability to create and adapt software directly, without waiting in line, filing tickets, or negotiating with vendors.
This isn’t a story about faster engineering teams. It’s a story about what happens when the power to build moves from departments to desks.
The Misaligned Metric Problem
We’ve become excellent at measuring the wrong things. Lines of code, PR counts, story points — these are proxies at best, distractions at worst. They optimize locally while the system remains constrained.
Consider the actual bottlenecks in most organizations: bureaucracy, coordination overhead, procurement cycles that stretch into quarters. An engineer can ship a feature in a day, but getting the business requirement clarified takes three weeks. A team can double their velocity, but the dependency on another team hasn’t changed.
AI amplifies this problem. Output increases, but impact doesn’t automatically follow. It’s like widening a highway that still feeds into a single toll booth. The throughput looks impressive until you notice the queue hasn’t moved.
Code Velocity vs Capability Velocity
The conceptual pivot we need is from code velocity to capability velocity.
Code velocity is team-centric and output-oriented. It’s easy to measure and frequently disconnected from actual business or individual value. It answers the question: how fast is the engineering organization producing artifacts?
Capability velocity is different. It’s individual-centric and leverage-oriented. It’s harder to measure but closer to real impact. It answers a better question: how effectively can a person remove friction and take action?
When we shift to capability velocity, personal software becomes visible as the primary mechanism of change.
The Rise of Personal Software
Personal software isn’t a product category. It’s a phenomenon.
These are small, purpose-built tools created by individuals or micro-teams: scripts that automate a tedious workflow, prompts that encode domain expertise, micro-agents that handle research, lightweight dashboards that surface exactly the data someone needs. Not products. Not platforms. Artifacts — built to serve a specific person’s specific context.
For decades, this kind of toolmaking was limited to engineers. You needed to write code, manage environments, understand infrastructure. The barrier was high enough that most people simply tolerated their software rather than shaping it.
The unlock happens when that changes. When software becomes malleable at the individual level, everyone becomes a potential toolmaker.
The Value of Personal Software
The value of personal software extends far beyond “saving time.” It creates leverage across multiple dimensions that compound over time.
Economic Leverage
Every personal tool that replaces a niche SaaS feature is money saved. Enterprise licenses for single features that someone actually needs, vendor contracts negotiated for capabilities that could be built in an afternoon — personal software cuts through this. It also creates optionality. You’re not locked into a vendor’s roadmap or pricing changes.
Interface Leverage
Generic software is built for the median user. Personal software fits exactly how and where a person works. The right information appears in the right place at the right time. Context switching drops. Cognitive overhead shrinks. The interface becomes invisible because it was shaped by the person using it.
Coordination Leverage
With AI agents, individuals can operate like small teams. But more importantly, personal software becomes a coordination surface. A script shared between colleagues is a form of micro-standardization without the bureaucracy of formal process. Artifacts encode agreements and make them executable.
Time-to-Solution Collapse
In traditional organizations, friction gets queued. A problem becomes a ticket, the ticket waits, eventually someone addresses it. Personal software collapses this cycle. Friction moves from “live with it” to “solve it now.” The mental carry cost of unresolved problems — that background hum of accumulated annoyances — decreases.
Long-Tail Problem Coverage
Vendors optimize for the median user because that’s where the market is. But high leverage often hides in the long tail — the edge cases, the niche workflows, the problems too specific to justify a product. Personal software optimizes for exactly these situations. What would never be worth building for a million users becomes worth building for one.
Ownership and Adaptability
Tools evolve with roles. As responsibilities shift, as contexts change, personal software can be reshaped rather than replaced. This creates a different relationship with technology: software as something crafted and maintained, not something endured. Agency increases. So does craftsmanship.
Data Proximity and Privacy
Personal software operates closer to source data. Fewer exports to third-party tools means fewer potential leaks. Sharing becomes intentional rather than structural. Control over information boundaries improves without requiring complex governance frameworks.
Skill and Literacy Compounding
Building small tools increases systems thinking. Someone who automates one workflow starts seeing automation opportunities everywhere. Software literacy rises organically. The organization gains technical intuition without formal training programs.
Innovation and Experimentation
Personal tools function as micro-labs. Ideas can be tested cheaply and locally before any decision about scaling. Many meaningful internal tools started as one person’s automation — a script that worked so well it spread.
Knowledge Externalization
Tacit knowledge is notoriously hard to transfer. But when someone builds a script or a prompt that encodes their expertise, that knowledge becomes an artifact. The automation doubles as documentation. Onboarding friction decreases. Knowledge silos weaken.
Morale and Motivation Effects
Solving your own friction is intrinsically rewarding. There’s a psychological shift from operator to builder, from someone who uses tools to someone who makes them. This counters the disengagement that comes from purely ticket-driven work, from being a cog in someone else’s system.
From Centralized Engineering to Distributed Problem Solving
The organizational implications are significant. Engineering doesn’t disappear — it transforms.
Instead of being the sole producer of software, engineering becomes the platform builder. The role shifts toward creating guardrails, providing tooling, curating context, ensuring security. Engineers multiply their reach instead of being bottlenecks.
The organization compounds instead of congesting. More problems get solved because more people can solve them. Engineering capacity focuses on the hard infrastructure while the long tail of small problems disperses across the people who understand them best.
The Artifact Layer
Personal software points toward a deeper shift in how organizations think about capability.
These artifacts — scripts, prompts, automations, dashboards — are durable capability units. They can be discovered, reused, and modified. They become shared memory and coordination primitives. Documentation stops being separate from software and becomes living software itself.
An organization that cultivates its artifact layer develops a different kind of institutional knowledge. Capability becomes tangible, searchable, and composable.
Risks, Friction, and Guardrails
Optimism should be balanced with realism.
Slop artifacts are a real risk. Low-barrier creation means low-barrier garbage — tools that break, that were never quite right, that accumulate without maintenance. Security and data leakage become concerns when anyone can pipe information through external APIs. Cost sprawl from unmanaged AI usage can surprise finance teams. Shadow IT dynamics — tools that bypass oversight entirely — create their own governance headaches.
But these are design and governance problems, not reasons for dismissal. Verification loops, clear policies, safe sandboxes, artifact review processes — these are solvable. The alternative, keeping software creation locked behind a single department, has its own costs. The question is how to enable without losing control, not whether to enable at all.
Measuring the Great Unlock
If we’re serious about capability velocity, we need different metrics. Output per team matters less than impact per person.
Some signals worth tracking: personal automations created per quarter, reduction in internal ticket queues, artifact reuse rates, time saved per individual task, cross-team artifact adoption, decrease in niche SaaS subscriptions. None of these are perfect, but they point toward what we actually care about — whether individuals are gaining leverage.
What Leadership Actually Needs To Do
The job of leadership is no longer just to fund software. It is to grow a workforce that can produce its own software.
Not everyone becomes a professional engineer. But everyone becomes AI-enabled and artifact-capable.
Upskill for Capability, Not Just Tools
Avoid the trap of “we bought licenses, we are done.” Tool access without capability building is wasted spend.
Training should focus on problem decomposition, prompting and agent orchestration, basic scripting and automation literacy, data awareness and verification habits. The goal is not feature familiarity. The goal is software agency — the ability to recognize a problem and build toward a solution.
Teach Personal Software Creation as a Core Skill
Position this like spreadsheet literacy in the 90s. Back then, the ability to build a model in Excel became a baseline expectation for knowledge workers. Personal software creation is the next version of that shift.
What does this look like in practice? Creating small automations. Building micro-dashboards. Writing task-specific scripts. Creating and refining AI agents. Encoding workflows into reusable artifacts. This is less “learn to code” and more “learn to shape tools.”
Provide Safe Sandboxes and Guardrails
Experimentation requires safety. Dedicated environments where things can break without consequence. Clear data boundaries and policies so people know what’s permissible. Pre-approved models and cost controls to prevent runaway spending. Templates, starter artifacts, and internal libraries to lower the barrier to starting.
The message should be: experiment freely, break safely.
Normalize Artifact Sharing
Individual gains become organizational compounding only when artifacts spread. This means building internal artifact libraries, cultivating lightweight review and reuse culture, celebrating small wins rather than only major launches.
Make “show your automation” as normal as “show your slide deck.” Visibility creates adoption. One person’s useful script becomes ten people’s useful script, but only if anyone knows it exists.
Redefine Engineering’s Role
Engineering doesn’t disappear — it transforms. Engineers become platform builders, toolsmiths, guardrail designers, internal consultants, context curators. They are not replaced. They become force multipliers, enabling capability across the organization rather than being the sole producers of software.
Align Incentives With Leverage
If incentives stay tied only to ticket throughput, the unlock stalls. Instead, reward automation that saves time. Recognize artifact reuse. Value problem elimination, not just task completion. Track capability growth, not only output metrics. What gets measured gets managed, and right now most organizations measure the wrong things.
Treat AI Spend as Capability Infrastructure
Think of this like cloud or network infrastructure — foundational, not optional. Budget for literacy and experimentation, not just licenses. Measure reduction in friction, SaaS sprawl, and queue time.
The question shifts from “How much does this cost?” to “What new capabilities did this unlock?”
The Leadership Bottom Line
The organizations that benefit most from AI will not be the ones with the biggest models. They will be the ones where the average employee can build small, useful software for themselves and share it with others. AI as a workforce capability multiplier, not just a productivity add-on.
A Phase Shift, Not an Optimization
The Great Unlock isn’t about producing more code. It’s about increasing the number of people who can shape software to their needs.
For decades, software was something that happened to most people. Developers built it, vendors sold it, everyone else adapted to it. The friction between how software worked and how people worked was accepted as normal.
That’s ending. When individuals gain software agency — the ability to create, adapt, and evolve their own tools — organizations unlock compounding leverage. Each person becomes a potential source of capability improvement. The long tail of small problems starts getting solved.
The future is not more software teams. The future is more people who can bend software to their needs.
